title_en: Affirming Allah's Attributes as They Appear in Texts
title_ar: إثبات صفات الله كما جاءت في النصوص
title_id: Menegaskan Sifat-Sifat Allah Sebagaimana Tertuang dalam Teks-Teks
content_id:
Di antara bab-bab terpenting al-Ibanah adalah pembahasan al-Asy'ari tentang sifat-sifat ilahi — sifat-sifat — sebagaimana terlihat dalam Al-Qur'an dan Sunnah yang autentik. Bab ini mencerminkan inti dari kembalinya teologisnya kepada metode ulama Salaf: menegaskan apa yang Allah katakan tentang diri-Nya tanpa penyimpangan, penolakan, atau penyerupaan dengan ciptaan.

Al-Qur'an mengandung banyak ayat di mana Allah menggambarkan diri-Nya menggunakan istilah-istilah yang memiliki makna yang jelas dalam bahasa Arab. Allah berbicara tentang Wajah-Nya (wajh), Tangan-Nya (yadayn), Mata-Nya ('ayn), istiwa'-Nya di atas 'Arsy, turunnya (nuzul), dan banyak karakteristik lain. Bagi ulama Salaf, respons yang benar terhadap teks-teks ini adalah membacanya sebagaimana adanya — menegaskan realitas yang mereka sampaikan sambil mengakui bahwa sifat-sifat ini tidak menyerupai sifat-sifat makhluk. Allah sendiri menetapkan prinsipnya: 'Tidak ada sesuatu pun yang serupa dengan Dia, dan Dia adalah Maha Mendengar lagi Maha Melihat' (al-Syura: 11).

Al-Asy'ari dalam al-Ibanah mengadopsi metodologi Salafi ini secara tegas. Ia menegaskan sifat-sifat yang disebutkan dalam Al-Qur'an dan Sunnah pada nilai zahirnya, tanpa terlibat dalam ta'wil (tafsir ulang) yang mencirikan kaum Mu'tazilah dan sebagian ulama kemudian. Ia menulis bahwa kaum Muslim pada masa awal menegaskan sifat-sifat ini dan meneruskannya tanpa bertanya 'bagaimana' (bila kayf), dan bahwa pengendalian diri ini bukan kegagalan intelektual melainkan ciri metode teologis yang benar.

Kontras dengan kaum Mu'tazilah sangat instruktif. Kaum Mu'tazilah mengingkari semua sifat yang mengimplikasikan keserupaan apa pun antara Allah dan ciptaan, mereduksi dzat ilahi menjadi keesaan telanjang yang ditelanjangi dari karakteristik apa pun yang dapat digambarkan. Pendekatan ini, yang dikenal sebagai ta'thil (pengosongan), dikritik oleh para ulama Sunni sebagai koreksi yang berlebihan ke arah yang salah. Mengatakan bahwa Allah tidak memiliki ilmu nyata, tidak memiliki kuasa nyata, tidak memiliki kalam nyata — hanya untuk menghindari implikasi yang tampak dari keserupaan dengan ciptaan — dipandang sebagai ekstrem yang terlalu jauh mengoreksi.

Al-Asy'ari mengemudi di antara ekstrem ta'thil dan ekstrem tamthil yang berlawanan (menyerupakan Allah dengan ciptaan). Cara keselamatan, ia tegaskan, adalah cara ulama Salaf: menegaskan sifat-sifat sebagaimana adanya dalam teks-teks, menerima maknanya dalam bahasa yang digunakan Allah dan Rasul-Nya, dan meninggalkan pertanyaan tentang hakikat akhirnya (kayfiyyah) kepada Allah semata. Sebagaimana Imam Malik berkata dengan terkenal ketika ditanya tentang istiva': 'Istiva' itu diketahui, caranya tidak diketahui, iman kepadanya wajib, dan bertanya tentangnya adalah bid'ah.'

Secara praktis, ini berarti ketika seorang Muslim membaca 'Yang Maha Pengasih beristiva' di atas 'Arsy' (Thaha: 5), mereka menegaskan istiva' yang nyata yang sesuai dengan keagungan Allah tanpa bertanya seperti apa istiva' itu atau membandingkannya dengan seorang raja yang naik ke singgasana. Ketika mereka membaca 'Tangan Allah di atas tangan mereka' (al-Fath: 10), mereka menegaskan Tangan yang nyata tanpa membandingkannya dengan tangan manusia atau bersikeras itu harus ditafsirkan sebagai kekuasaan atau kebaikan.

Penegasan ulang al-Asy'ari tentang metode ini dalam al-Ibanah adalah momen penting dalam sejarah teologi Islam. Ia menunjukkan bahwa bahkan seorang ahli kalam rasional, yang terlatih dalam mazhab Mu'tazilah, dapat mengakui keunggulan metode tekstual ulama Salaf ketika dihadapkan pada bobot penuh bukti Al-Qur'an dan kenabian.
content_en:
Among the most important chapters of Al-Ibanah is al-Ash'ari's treatment of the divine attributes — the sifat — as they appear in the Quran and the authentic Sunnah. This chapter reflects the heart of his theological return to the method of the Salaf: affirming what Allah said about Himself without distortion, denial, or likening to creation.

The Quran contains many verses in which Allah describes Himself using terms that carry obvious meaning in Arabic. Allah speaks of His Face (wajh), His Hands (yadayn), His Eye (ayn), His rising over the Throne (istiwa), His descent (nuzul), and many other characteristics. For the Salaf, the correct response to these texts was to read them as they came — affirming the reality they convey while acknowledging that these attributes do not resemble the attributes of created beings. Allah Himself established the principle: 'There is nothing like Him, and He is the All-Hearing, the All-Seeing' (al-Shura: 11).

Al-Ash'ari in Al-Ibanah adopts this Salafi methodology explicitly. He affirms the attributes mentioned in the Quran and Sunnah at face value, without engaging in the ta'wil (reinterpretation) that characterized the Mu'tazilah and some later scholars. He writes that the Muslims of the early period affirmed these attributes and transmitted them without asking 'how' (bila kayf), and that this restraint was not a failure of intellect but a mark of sound theological method.

The contrast with the Mu'tazilah is instructive. The Mu'tazilah denied all attributes that implied any resemblance between Allah and creation, reducing the divine essence to a bare unity stripped of any describable characteristics. This approach, known as ta'til (stripping or emptying), was criticized by the Sunni scholars as effectively denying Allah's reality. To say that Allah has no real knowledge, no real power, no real speech — only to avoid the apparent implication of resemblance to creation — was seen as an extreme that overcorrected in the wrong direction.

Al-Ash'ari steers between the extreme of ta'til and the opposite extreme of tamthil (comparing Allah to creation). The way of safety, he affirms, is the way of the Salaf: affirming the attributes as they come in the texts, accepting their meanings in the language used by Allah and His Messenger, and leaving aside the question of their ultimate nature (kayfiyyah) to Allah alone. As Imam Malik famously said when asked about the istiwa: 'The istiwa is known, the how is unknown, belief in it is obligatory, and asking about it is an innovation.'

Practically, this means that when a Muslim reads 'The All-Merciful rose over the Throne' (Ta-Ha: 5), they affirm a real rising (istiwa) that befits Allah's majesty without asking what that rising looks like or comparing it to a king ascending a throne. When they read 'The Hand of Allah is over their hands' (al-Fath: 10), they affirm a real Hand without comparing it to a human hand or insisting it must be interpreted as power or blessing.

Al-Ash'ari's reaffirmation of this method in Al-Ibanah was a significant moment in the history of Islamic theology. It demonstrated that even a master of rational kalam, trained in the Mu'tazilite school, could recognize the superiority of the textual method of the Salaf when confronted with the full weight of Quranic and prophetic evidence.
content_ar:
Among the most important chapters of Al-Ibanah is al-Ash'ari's treatment of the divine attributes — the sifat — as they appear in the Quran and the authentic Sunnah. This chapter reflects the heart of his theological return to the method of the Salaf: affirming what الله said about Himself without distortion, denial, or likening to creation.

The Quran contains many verses in which الله describes Himself using terms that carry obvious meaning in Arabic. الله speaks of His Face (wajh), His Hands (yadayn), His Eye (ayn), His rising over the Throne (istiwa), His descent (nuzul), and many other characteristics. For the Salaf, the correct response to these texts was to read them as they came — affirming the reality they convey while acknowledging that these attributes do not resemble the attributes of created beings. الله Himself established the principle: 'There is nothing like Him, and He is the All-Hearing, the All-Seeing' (al-Shura: 11).

Al-Ash'ari in Al-Ibanah adopts this Salafi methodology explicitly. He affirms the attributes mentioned in the Quran and Sunnah at face value, without engaging in the ta'wil (reinterpretation) that characterized the Mu'tazilah and some later scholars. He writes that the Muslims of the early period affirmed these attributes and transmitted them without asking 'how' (bila kayf), and that this restraint was not a failure of intellect but a mark of sound theological method.

The contrast with the Mu'tazilah is instructive. The Mu'tazilah denied all attributes that implied any resemblance between الله and creation, reducing the divine essence to a bare unity stripped of any describable characteristics. This approach, known as ta'til (stripping or emptying), was criticized by the Sunni scholars as effectively denying الله's reality. To say that الله has no real knowledge, no real power, no real speech — only to avoid the apparent implication of resemblance to creation — was seen as an extreme that overcorrected in the wrong direction.

Al-Ash'ari steers between the extreme of ta'til and the opposite extreme of tamthil (comparing الله to creation). The way of safety, he affirms, is the way of the Salaf: affirming the attributes as they come in the texts, accepting their meanings in the language used by الله and His Messenger, and leaving aside the question of their ultimate nature (kayfiyyah) to الله alone. As Imam Malik famously said when asked about the istiwa: 'The istiwa is known, the how is unknown, belief in it is obligatory, and asking about it is an innovation.'

Practically, this means that when a Muslim reads 'The All-Merciful rose over the Throne' (Ta-Ha: 5), they affirm a real rising (istiwa) that befits الله's majesty without asking what that rising looks like or comparing it to a king ascending a throne.
